target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
  "plugins": { "loaded": ["acpx", "browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "openai", "phone-control", "talk-voice"], "errors": [] }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000
{
  "ok": true,
  "enqueued": true,
  "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2"
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "id": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
  "name": "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof",
  "delivery": { "mode": "none", "channel": "last" },
  "state": {
    "lastRunStatus": "ok",
    "lastStatus": "ok",
    "lastDurationMs": 14308,
    "lastDeliveryStatus": "not-requested",
    "consecutiveErrors": 0
  }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "jobId": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
      "action": "finished",
      "status": "ok",
      "summary": "OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK",
      "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2",
      "durationMs": 14308,
      "model": "gpt-5.5",
      "provider": "codex-cli",
      "delivered": false,
      "deliveryStatus": "not-requested"
    }
  ],
  "total": 1,
  "hasMore": false
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real local gateway accepted the cron job, ran it through the registered `codex-cli` backend, recovered `OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK` during the repair flow, and recorded the run as `status: "ok"`. Delivery was not requested by design because the proof command used `--no-deliver`; the current code keeps the CLI mode explicit, resumes the first CLI session for repair, and does not hit the earlier native-tool fail-closed error.

What was not tested: External WhatsApp/Telegram send was not exercised; delivery was disabled to keep the local proof isolated and non-invasive.
Current-head ownership, lint, and Greptile cleanup verification after commit `d6ed463c96`:

```text
# RED before the ownership fix:
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
FAIL passes senderIsOwner=false to external-hook CLI cron runs
AssertionError: expected true to be false

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
FAIL preserves external-hook non-owner scope during CLI empty-output repair
AssertionError: expected true to be false

# GREEN after the ownership fix:
$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       5 passed (5)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       9 passed (9)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       20 passed (20)

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/cli-runner/types.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

corepack pnpm check:changed
$ node scripts/check-changed.mjs

git diff --check
# clean
```

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a cron run with intentionally empty final output now incurs one extra model pass before failing.
  - Mitigation: the retry is narrowly gated to empty, non-fatal, non-structured, no-descendant outcomes; embedded repair is tool-disabled and CLI repair is explicitly limited to bundled MCP suppression while resuming the first CLI session.
- Risk: CLI repair mode could accidentally preserve bundled MCP wiring.
  - Mitigation: `disableTools` now disables bundled MCP preparation, covered by `src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`.

target.title: docs(memory): add memory system deep dive guide
target.body:
## Summary

Add a comprehensive memory system deep dive guide, addressing #43032.

### Problem

`docs/concepts/memory.md` covers basics (~600 words) but lacks depth on memory best practices, search optimization, lifecycle management, and advanced patterns.

### New document: `docs/concepts/memory-deep-dive.md` (401 lines)

Covers all 6 sections requested in the issue:

1. **Memory Architecture** — Two-layer model (`MEMORY.md` + `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`), indexing scope, what is searchable vs not
2. **Writing Effective Memory** — What belongs in MEMORY.md vs daily logs, promotion workflow, formatting tips for better search indexing
3. **Search Optimization** — Hybrid retrieval pipeline (keyword + vector + MMR), query strategies (semantic vs exact), `memory_search` → `memory_get` workflow, tuning parameters (`maxResults`, `minScore`)
4. **Memory Maintenance** — Heartbeat-based periodic review, cleanup strategies, `heartbeat-state.json` tracking
5. **Advanced Patterns** — Entity tracking with stable headings, opinion evolution with timestamps, cross-session recall via `sessionMemory`
6. **Troubleshooting** — No results, keyword-only matches, `memory_get` file restrictions, MEMORY.md bloat, missing transcript search

### Navigation

Added to `docs.json` under Sessions and memory → Memory group, right after `concepts/memory`.

### Source references

- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts` — default settings (maxResults: 6, maxSnippetChars: 700)
- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/read-file.ts` — `memory_get` file boundary enforcement
- `src/agents/memory-search.ts` — search config resolution and sources
- `docs/concepts/memory-builtin.md` — existing builtin engine docs

Closes #43032

target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: - N/A - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - N/A - Why this regressed now: - N/A - If unknown, what was ruled out: - N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: * The Linux companion introduces a new native desktop surface that can read local runtime context, inspect service state, connect to the local gateway over HTTP/WebSocket, edit config, open local logs/transcripts when available, and trigger gateway-backed management mutations. * Risk is mitigated by keeping the runtime model explicit, separating systemd discovery from gateway RPC transport, validating config JSON before save, preserving unrelated config content during rebuild, using `baseHash` on config writes, hardening request shapes through typed mutation helpers, and covering lifecycle/parsing/request-shape behavior in targeted tests. * Local-vs-remote resource access is handled explicitly so the app does not blindly assume every gateway-returned path is locally openable. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment * OS: * Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME * Runtime/container: * Bare-metal / local user-session workflow with systemd user service * Model/provider: * Local gateway-backed companion flow; model/provider depends on gateway config * Integration/channel (if any): * systemd user service, HTTP health, authenticated gateway WebSocket, GTK4 + Libadwaita, Ayatana/AppIndicator * Relevant config (redacted): * Fresh local OpenClaw config and user `openclaw-gateway.service`; secrets redacted ### Steps 1. Install Linux build dependencies and clone/build OpenClaw. 2. Build and run the Linux app with Meson. 3. Launch the Linux companion app before running `openclaw setup`. 4. Run `openclaw setup`, then relaunch or refresh the companion app. 5. Install and start the user gateway service. 6. Launch or refresh the Linux companion app again. 7. Exercise tray/status, diagnostics, onboarding/readiness, and the Dashboard / General / Channels / Skills / Sessions / Cron / Instances sections. 8. Run the Linux test suite. ### Expected * Before setup, the app reports the missing setup/runtime state clearly. * After setup but before gateway install, the app reports configured-but-not-installed service state clearly. * After gateway install/start, the app reports a healthy connected state, with diagnostics, dashboard, and general surfaces reflecting the running gateway. * The Linux app builds, launches, connects through the native runtime model, reflects systemd/gateway state accurately, and exposes native management flows for the major gateway-backed sections. * The Linux test suite passes. ### Actual * Matches expected in local verification. ## Evidence Attach at least one: * [x] Failing test/log before + passing after * [x] Trace/log snippets * [x] Screenshot/recording * [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What I personally verified (not just CI), and how: * Verified scenarios: * Built the Linux app from a clean checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME. * Ran the Meson test suite locally. * Verified tray helper spawn and tray/status behavior. * Verified systemd user-service discovery and lifecycle reflection. * Verified diagnostics/readiness/runtime presentation. * Verified first-run progression manually with screenshots: * app launched before `openclaw setup` * app after `openclaw setup` * app after gateway install/start with the gateway answering properly * Verified the healthy connected state manually against a fresh configured local gateway. * Verified additional Linux app surfaces manually, including Dashboard, General, and Diagnostics. * Verified the main Linux companion window and controller-backed sections load and refresh. * Verified native management flows across Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances at the implementation level. * Verified config editing flow behavior, including full-document rebuild and hash-aware save path. * Verified locality-aware log/transcript fallback behavior. * Edge cases checked: * Invalid config JSON aborts save. * Config rebuild preserves unrelated top-level keys and sibling channel config. * Mutation request shapes match the intended backend contract structure. * RPC lifecycle handling covers success/error/timeout/disconnect cleanup paths. * Tray-helper lifecycle and cleanup behavior were tightened. * Readiness states remain intelligible across the bootstrap sequence from no setup -> configured -> installed/running. * What I did **not** verify: * Official distro packaging artifacts (`.deb`, `.rpm`, Snap) are not included here. * Broad desktop-environment compatibility beyond the Ubuntu GNOME target path. * Full end-to-end parity for every macOS companion capability. ## Review Conversations * [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. * [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. If none, write `None`. * Risk: * Linux desktop behavior can vary by distro and shell, especially around tray hosting. * Mitigation: * Target the initial implementation clearly at Ubuntu GNOME, use a private AppIndicator helper, and keep the helper boundary isolated from the GTK4 main process. * Risk: * The Linux companion now depends on a larger native runtime stack: systemd context, HTTP health, WebSocket RPC, and gateway payload parsing. * Mitigation: * Keep those concerns separated into dedicated modules and cover parser/RPC/mutation behavior with focused tests. * Risk: * Config editing could accidentally overwrite unrelated config content if the save path is too narrow. * Mitigation: * Load the full config document, edit only the relevant subtree, rebuild the full document, validate JSON before save, and send `baseHash` with `config.set`. * Risk: * Transcript/log opening can fail or behave incorrectly if the gateway resource is not local to the GTK host. * Mitigation: * Enforce locality-aware fallback logic and only open local resources directly when they are actually present and accessible. * Risk: * This is a large first-class app addition and therefore a large review surface. * Mitigation: * Keep the app organized into focused subsystems and section controllers, provide a documented Linux build/test path, and include targeted tests for the most contract-sensitive areas.

target.title: Add user chat bubble color selector for macOS application
target.body: # Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: The macOS chat bubble color is hardcoded to a static blue, with no way for users to personalize it. - Why it matters: Users want visual customization of the chat interface to match their preferences or distinguish between instances. - What changed: Added a new "Colors" settings tab with a 10-color palette picker, a custom `ColorPicker`, and a system accent color toggle. `ColorPreferencesStore` (in `OpenClawKit`) persists preferences via `UserDefaults` and exposes a `resolvedColor` that `ChatTheme.userBubble` now reads on macOS. `userText` dynamically picks black or white based on WCAG relative luminance. A cross-platform `ColorHexSupport` utility was added to `OpenClawKit` for hex-to-Color and Color-to-hex conversions. The macOS-local `ColorHexSupport` gained a reverse `hex(from:)` helper. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): iOS chat bubble color remains the existing static blue. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - macOS: New "Colors" tab in Settings (between "Channels" and "Skills") with three selection modes: system accent color (default, preserves existing behavior), palette color (10 curated colors from Apple's Human Interface Guidelines), or arbitrary custom color via `ColorPicker`. - Chat bubble color updates live when a new color is selected; preferences persist across app restarts via `UserDefaults`. - User message text automatically switches between white and black depending on the chosen bubble color's luminance (WCAG threshold at 0.6). ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user message] -> [hardcoded rgb(127,184,212) bubble] -> [white text] After: [user message] -> [ColorPreferencesStore.resolvedColor] -> [palette / custom / accent bubble] -> [WCAG luminance > 0.6?] -> yes -> [black text] -> no -> [white text] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS 15+ - Runtime/container: Swift 6 / SwiftUI - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Build and launch the macOS app. 2. Open Settings > Colors. 3. Select a palette color, apply a custom color, or toggle "Use Accent Color." 4. Open a chat session see the selected color on your messages. ### Expected - User chat bubble reflects the selected color. - Text is white on dark bubbles and black on light bubbles (e.g. yellow). - Preference survives app restart. ### Actual - Verified as expected. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) **Color palette** **Custom color** **Dark text on bright color** ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Palette selection, custom color via ColorPicker, accent color toggle, preference persistence across restart, live bubble color update in chat. - Edge cases checked: Light colors (yellow, orange) produce black text; dark colors (indigo, blue) produce white text. Clearing custom color falls back to palette or accent. Off-main-thread `resolvedColor` defaults to light palette variant. - What you did **not** verify: iOS runtime (feature is macOS-only for now). No automated Swift tests were added for the new views or store. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: `ColorPreferencesStore.shared` is `nonisolated(unsafe)` and initialized eagerly in `AppState.init`; concurrent first access from a background thread could race. - Mitigation: `AppState` calls `_ = ColorPreferencesStore.shared` during init on the main actor, ensuring the singleton is created before any background reader. - Risk: `UserDefaults`-backed preferences do not sync across macOS reinstalls or profiles. - Mitigation: Defaults to system accent color (existing behavior); preferences are non-critical and easily re-set.

target.title: fix(hooks): restore --exclude-labels in Gmail watcher args
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #56635

- Restores `--exclude-labels SPAM,TRASH,DRAFT,SENT` in `gog gmail watch serve` args so sent mail and draft saves do not trigger Gmail hook runs by default.
- Adds `hooks.gmail.excludeLabels` to the config schema/types with runtime default resolution and hook override support.
- Updates the sanitized log-args expectation so `--exclude-labels` appears in non-secret watcher logs.
- Regenerates the base config schema and docs baseline hash, and documents the default excluded label set.

AI-assisted: yes (Codex). I reviewed the diff and verified the behavior locally.

target.title: feat(extension): support session cache for volcengine
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Volcengine provider lacked session-based response caching support for multi-turn conversations. - Why it matters: Without session caching, every turn sends full context to the API, increasing latency and cost for Volcengine Doubao models. - What changed: Added extensions/volcengine/cache.ts with session cache logic — manages previous_response_id, caching, thinking, store, and expire_at fields automatically. Configured via params.cache: { enable: true, ttlSec?, thinking? }. Wired into the provider via wrapStreamFn in index.ts. (cache.test.ts, 27 tests). Updated docs with config examples. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Provider registration, catalog, auth flow unchanged. No changes outside extensions/volcengine/ and docs. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). Once enable cache and openai-responses, we can see the cache read token from session file for each LLM request config example： ``` "models": { "providers": { "volcengine": { "api": "openai-responses" } } }, "agents": { "defaults": { "models": { "volcengine/xxxx": { "alias": "volcengine", "params": { "cache": { "enable": false, "ttlSec": 3600, "thinking": true } } } } } } ``` session file example: ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? target.title: feat(acp): forward image blocks in tool call results
target.body:
## Summary

- `extractToolCallContent()` in the ACP event mapper previously only extracted `type: "text"` blocks from tool results, silently dropping image content blocks
- ACP clients (Feishu, Discord, etc.) could not display images produced by tools (e.g. the Read tool reading PNG/JPG files) because image blocks were never forwarded
- Added image block extraction with validation (`data` and `mimeType` must both be present), using the existing `ImageContent` type from `@agentclientprotocol/sdk`
- No protocol changes needed — the ACP SDK already supports `ImageContent` in `ToolCallContent`

## Test plan

- [x] Added unit tests for `extractToolCallContent()` covering:
  - Text-only extraction (existing behavior)
  - Image-only extraction
  - Mixed text + image blocks
  - Malformed image blocks (missing data/mimeType) are skipped
  - Empty content returns undefined
  - String input fallback
- [ ] Manual verification: use ACP bridge with a Feishu/Discord client, have the agent read a PNG file, confirm the image is displayed in the client

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(agents): hint when unknown tools are skills
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** When a model mistakenly calls a skill name as a tool (e.g. `my-skill`), the error message is a generic "Tool not found" with no guidance, so the model keeps retrying or gives up. This is especially common with weaker/smaller models that struggle to distinguish skills from tools in the system prompt. - **Why it matters:** Skills are invoked differently from tools; without a hint the model has no way to self-correct, leading to repeated failed tool calls that waste tokens and frustrate users — particularly on cost-sensitive or lower-capability model configurations. - **What changed:** Register an `afterToolCall` hook in `attempt.ts` that intercepts error tool results, detects unknown-tool errors matching a registered skill name, and rewrites the message to `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` Also extracted `resolveUnknownToolName` helper in `errors.ts` (was inline regex) and exported `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` for the hook to call. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** `formatAssistantErrorText` signature and all its callers are untouched. No new params threaded through `run.ts`, `payloads.ts`, `subscribe.types.ts`, or lifecycle handlers. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Skills / tool execution ## Linked Issue/PR None ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - When a model calls a skill name as a tool and the skill is registered, the error tool result now says: `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` - When the tool name does not match any registered skill, behavior is unchanged (generic "Tool not found"). ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64) - Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm - Model/provider: Claude (Anthropic) - Relevant config: workspace with registered skills (e.g. `my-skill`, `another-skill`) ### Steps 1. Start an OpenClaw agent session with skills registered 2. Prompt the model to call a skill name as if it were a tool (e.g. "use tool my-skill") 3. Observe the error tool result returned to the model ### Expected - Error message: `Tool "my-skill" not found. "my-skill" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` ### Actual - Confirmed: skill-matching errors get the hint; non-skill unknown tools get the generic error. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets Manually tested two scenarios: 1. Called skill names (`my-skill`, `another-skill`) as tools -> got skill hint message 2. Called nonexistent name (`banana123`) as tool -> got generic "Tool not found" ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Both skill-match and non-match unknown tool errors in a live OpenClaw agent session. - **Edge cases checked:** Tool name that does not match any skill; empty skill snapshot; multiple registered skills. - **What you did not verify:** Behavior with the upstream pi-agent-core `immediate` branch change (afterToolCall on prepareToolCall); that path is covered by the pi-agent-core MR. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit `e527e3c111`; the `afterToolCall` hook is additive and removing it restores default unknown-tool errors. - Files/config to restore: `errors.ts`, `attempt.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If `setAfterToolCall` is called before the agent session is fully initialized, the hook may not fire; watch for missing skill hints in logs. ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` regex may not match all provider error formats. - **Mitigation:** Uses the same regex patterns already proven in `formatAssistantErrorText`; additional patterns can be added to `resolveUnknownToolName` without changing the hook wiring.

target.title: feat(session): add chat-channel session switching with LRU history retention
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Chat channels did not support listing or switching between prior sessions under the same SessionKey, and follow-up fixes were needed to preserve switchable transcripts until LRU eviction and restore per-session usage/compaction snapshots correctly.
- Why it matters: Without in-chat session switching, users cannot navigate past conversations from Feishu/DingTalk/Telegram/Discord-style chat surfaces; incorrect transcript/archive or snapshot behavior would also make `/session back`, `/sessions`, and `/status`-adjacent state feel unreliable.
- What changed: Added `/sessions`, `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for chat channels; introduced per-SessionKey LRU `sessionHistory`; preserved switchable transcripts until history eviction; restored token/context/compaction snapshots on switch; documented matching/order/retention behavior; cleaned up reset semantics so `session.historyLimit=0` truly disables switchable history.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change SessionKey routing, does not add cross-SessionKey switching, does not add named sessions/UI picker flows, and does not change channel plugin-specific transport behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #9959
- Related #28168

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Added `/sessions` to list switchable sessions for the current chat SessionKey.
- Added `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for in-chat session switching.
- Current session is included in `/sessions`, marked as `[current]`.
- `/sessions` now shows compact single-line preview rows with session ID prefix and truncated preview text.
- Session switching now restores stored token/context/compaction snapshots for the target session.
- `session.historyLimit` now consistently supports `0` as “disable switchable session history”.
- Transcripts for sessions still in history remain switchable; transcripts are archived when evicted from the LRU history queue.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw dev runtime / source tree
- Model/provider: N/A for core session-switch logic
- Integration/channel (if any): chat-channel command flow; manually validated in a real chat session
- Relevant config (redacted):
  - `session.historyLimit: 5` (default path)
  - `session.historyLimit: 0` (disable-history regression path)

### Steps

1. Start a chat conversation and create multiple sessions with `/new`.
2. Run `/sessions`, then switch via `/session <number>` or `/session back`.
3. Continue creating sessions until the history limit is exceeded.

### Expected

- `/sessions` lists current + recent switchable sessions in LRU order.
- Switching restores the target session’s stored metadata/snapshots.
- Sessions still present in history remain switchable.
- The oldest session is archived only after LRU eviction.
- With `session.historyLimit=0`, switchable history is disabled and the previous session is archived immediately on reset.

### Actual

- Behavior matches the expected results above in targeted tests and manual validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `/sessions` output in chat
  - `/session back` restores the previous session
  - switching to a still-retained historical session restores expected context/state
  - LRU eviction archives only the oldest evicted session
  - daily reset path preserves switchable history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` disables switchable history and archives immediately on reset
- Edge cases checked:
  - history boundary behavior at the configured limit
  - selecting current session is a no-op
  - unique-prefix SessionId selection behavior
  - stale/daily-reset session rollover
- What you did **not** verify:
  - every individual chat provider/plugin surface
  - cross-SessionKey switching (out of scope)
  - any UI/card-based session picker (out of scope)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - revert this PR, or set `session.historyLimit=0` to disable switchable session history
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`
  - `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
  - `src/config/types.base.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - `/sessions` shows stale/incorrect ordering
  - switched sessions lose expected token/context/compaction snapshot state
  - transcripts are archived too early while still present in history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` does not disable switchable history

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: LRU eviction/archive timing could regress and archive a session that should still remain switchable.
  - Mitigation: Added/updated targeted tests covering retained-history vs evicted-history behavior.

- Risk: Snapshot restore could leave token/context/compaction state inconsistent after switching.
  - Mitigation: Added lifecycle tests covering restore and missing-metadata fallback behavior.

- Risk: Docs could drift from implementation again as follow-up fixes land.
  - Mitigation: Updated `docs/design/chat-session-switch.md` to match the current implementation semantics in this PR.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted: Yes

I used an AI assistant for code review, doc cleanup, and targeted test updates. I manually reviewed the final diff, understood the changes, and verified the session-switch flows myself.

target.title: fix(security): use constant-time comparison for TLS fingerprint verification
target.body:
## Summary

TLS certificate fingerprint verification in `src/gateway/client.ts` uses `!==` (standard JavaScript string equality), which is vulnerable to **timing side-channel attacks** (CWE-208).

An attacker on the network path can measure response time differences to iteratively guess the correct fingerprint byte-by-byte, potentially bypassing TLS pinning for MITM attacks on gateway connections.

## Root cause

Two locations perform fingerprint comparison with `!==`:

| Location | Context |
|----------|---------|
| Line 205 | WebSocket `checkServerIdentity` callback |
| Line 681 | HTTP `validateTlsFingerprint` method |

## Fix

Replace both with `safeEqualSecret()` from `src/security/secret-equal.ts`, which uses `SHA-256` hashing + `crypto.timingSafeEqual` for constant-time comparison.

This is the **same function already used** for pairing token verification elsewhere in the codebase — just missed for TLS fingerprints.

```diff
- if (fingerprint !== expected) {
+ if (!safeEqualSecret(fingerprint, expected)) {
```

## Why safeEqualSecret and not raw timingSafeEqual

`timingSafeEqual` requires both buffers to have equal length, which would leak the length of the expected fingerprint. `safeEqualSecret` avoids this by hashing both values with SHA-256 first (producing fixed 32-byte digests), then comparing the digests in constant time.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo` — compiles cleanly
- [x] `pnpm check` — lint/format passes
- [x] Existing `safeEqualSecret` tests in `src/security/audit-extra.sync.test.ts` cover equal, unequal, different-length, and undefined inputs
- [x] No remaining `fingerprint !== expected` patterns in codebase

target.title: Rescue: add watchdog core service and cron engine
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had no built-in isolated rescue watchdog that could detect an unhealthy managed gateway profile and repair it without going through the failing primary session.
- Why it matters: the larger rescue-watchdog feature is hard to review as one PR; this split lands the core runtime/service layer first so maintainers can review the actual watchdog mechanism without onboarding/UI noise.
- What changed: adds daemon/service hardening needed by the watchdog, introduces a new isolated `rescueWatchdog` cron payload + runner, and wires the gateway cron service to execute that watchdog job against a monitored profile.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no onboarding wizard flow, no CLI `openclaw onboard --rescue-watchdog` UX, and no docs changes in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #31480
- Supersedes #44113
- Supersedes #46493
- Supersedes #46499

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adds a new internal isolated cron payload kind, `rescueWatchdog`, for watchdog jobs that monitor a target profile and repair it when unhealthy.
- Hardens LaunchAgent plist writes: trusted-home resolution, symlink rejection, atomic writes, and `0600` plist permissions.
- Managed service restart helpers now accept `AbortSignal`, so watchdog restart attempts can be canceled cleanly instead of hanging until command timeouts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  The new capability is intentionally narrow: an isolated cron job can probe and repair a monitored local profile. Risk is bounded by restricting the payload shape, disallowing delivery targets for `rescueWatchdog`, resolving the monitored profile explicitly, and hardening the underlying managed-service restart and launchd plist write paths. The repair fallback uses exact argv via `process.execPath` and avoids shell-based PATH resolution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): local managed gateway service / cron service only

### Steps

1. Run the daemon/unit tests covering launchd, systemd, schtasks, and process-exec timeout behavior.
2. Run the cron/rescue unit tests covering payload normalization, job validation, watchdog execution, and gateway cron wiring.
3. Build the repo and validate config to confirm the combined core layer compiles and loads cleanly.

### Expected

- A `rescueWatchdog` cron job can be normalized, stored, validated, and executed as an isolated job.
- Managed service restart helpers are abortable and launchd plist writes fail closed on unsafe targets.

### Actual

- Verified by targeted tests and local build/config validation; behavior matches the expected outcomes above.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - launchd plist path resolution ignores a conflicting `HOME` override, rejects symlink targets, and writes plist files with `0600` permissions via temp-file + rename.
  - `AbortSignal` now propagates through launchd/systemd/schtasks restart helpers into the process exec layer.
  - `rescueWatchdog` cron payloads normalize correctly, are forced to isolated session targets, and reject delivery/failureDestination config.
  - the rescue watchdog runner probes the monitored profile, attempts managed service restart, falls back to `doctor --repair --non-interactive`, and reports success/error summaries correctly.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `os.userInfo()` failure falls back to `os.homedir()`, and unresolved trusted home fails closed.
  - monitored rescue-profile names are rejected.
  - restart/repair flows respect cron timeout budget and abort behavior.
  - `launchd` temp plist files are cleaned up if rename fails.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - manual end-to-end watchdog repair on a real long-running host with an actually failing managed gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/daemon/*`, `src/process/exec.ts`, `src/cron/*`, `src/rescue/watchdog-shared.ts`, `src/gateway/server-cron.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: launchd install unexpectedly failing on valid paths, or isolated cron jobs rejecting valid `agentTurn` jobs after the new payload kind was added.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LaunchAgent path hardening could reject previously tolerated but unsafe filesystem layouts.
  - Mitigation: the checks are limited to symlinked/non-directory targets in the LaunchAgents path and have focused test coverage.
- Risk: adding a new cron payload kind could accidentally loosen validation or delivery behavior.
  - Mitigation: `rescueWatchdog` is explicitly restricted to isolated jobs, delivery is rejected, and schema/service/normalization paths have dedicated tests.
- Risk: watchdog repair code could leave stuck restart attempts behind.
  - Mitigation: abort propagation is threaded through the restart backends and the watchdog enforces bounded restart/repair timeouts.

target.title: security: create session dirs with private permissions
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories were created with default permissions, which landed as `0755` on POSIX hosts in practice.
- Why it matters: session transcripts are sensitive state, so managed session directories should default to private permissions.
- What changed: added `ensurePrivateSessionsDir(...)` for managed session directories and reused it across setup, onboarding, agent creation, and managed session store / transcript flows.
- Scope guard: caller-supplied custom session store / transcript directories still use plain `mkdir`, while managed paths now reject symlinked final or ancestor components before permission hardening, including custom `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` roots.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories are now created as private directories (`0700` on POSIX, best-effort equivalent on Windows). Caller-supplied custom session store / transcript directories keep their previous plain-`mkdir` behavior.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.6.1
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.0, pnpm 10.23.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): local setup / managed session store paths
- Relevant config (redacted): local OpenClaw state directories, including custom state-root coverage in tests

### Steps

1. Run setup or create a fresh managed session store under `agents/<id>/sessions/`.
2. Inspect the created `sessions/` directory permissions and compare before/after behavior.
3. Repeat with custom caller-supplied store / transcript paths to confirm they still use plain directory creation.
4. Exercise symlinked managed-path edge cases.

### Expected

- Managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories are created with private permissions (`0700` on POSIX, best-effort equivalent on Windows).
- Custom caller-supplied directories are not re-permissioned.
- Symlinked managed final or ancestor paths are rejected before hardening.

### Actual

- Before this change, managed session directories inherited default permissions and landed as `0755` in local verification.
- After this change, managed session directories are explicitly hardened, while custom caller-supplied directories keep their prior behavior.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified locally:
  - `pnpm test -- src/config/sessions/paths.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/commands/setup.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked:
  - managed session directories are hardened to private permissions on POSIX
  - Windows permission assertions stay best-effort in tests
  - symlinked managed final or ancestor paths are rejected in the session-path helper tests
  - custom caller-supplied store / transcript directories are not forced to `0700`
- What you did **not** verify locally:
  - full repo `pnpm check`
  - full cross-platform onboarding E2E flows

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR's commit stack
- Files/config to restore: `src/config/sessions/paths.ts` and the managed session-directory callers
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected failures when managed session paths resolve through symlinked ancestors or unusual filesystems with restricted permission APIs

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: some filesystems may not honor the permission hardening path consistently.
  - Mitigation: the helper applies best-effort hardening and tests cover the intended managed-path behavior on supported platforms.
- Risk: broadening hardening to caller-supplied custom paths would be a behavioral regression.
  - Mitigation: custom store / transcript paths stay on plain `mkdir`; only managed session paths are hardened.

target.title: fix(ios): resolve main-thread warnings for CLLocationManager and SFSpeechRecognizer
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `GatewayConnectionController.currentPermissions()` triggered iOS UI-responsiveness warnings by repeatedly constructing `CLLocationManager` and querying speech authorization in a main-actor-sensitive way.
- Why it matters: repeated main-thread warnings are a signal of avoidable UI responsiveness risk in a frequently queried permission snapshot path.
- What changed: reused a single `CLLocationManager` instance, introduced a nonisolated speech-status accessor, and added tests around the updated permission snapshot behavior.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no permission semantics changed, no gateway protocol changed, and no new device capabilities were introduced.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: locally built, device-tested, plus iOS unit-test coverage added.

- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): none required beyond the controller’s existing permission-snapshot implementation.
- Why this regressed now: iOS/Xcode warning surfaces became more visible/strict for these framework access patterns.

- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover controller logic, while device verification confirms the warning symptom is gone in the real runtime.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): controller tests existed but were expanded because prior coverage did not assert the warning-prone path strongly enough.

target.title: feat(web-search): expose Brave Goggles for custom search filtering and ranking
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Brave Search supports [Goggles](https://search.brave.com/help/goggles), a [query-time DSL](https://brave.com/static-assets/files/goggles.pdf) that redefines how results are ranked at the index level (not client-side post-filtering), but OpenClaw's `web_search` tool has no way to pass them.
- Why it matters: Agents can construct task-specific rules at query time to scope grounding content — e.g. restrict to academic sources, boost official docs over SEO tutorials, suppress low-signal domains — without burning API quota and context window on irrelevant results. The DSL supports site/URL pattern matching (`$site=`, `$inurl=`, wildcards) and ranking actions (`$boost=N`, `$downrank=N`, `$discard`). Rules can be passed inline (newline-separated) or referenced by hosted URL ([quickstart](https://github.com/brave/goggles-quickstart)). Goggles work on both Brave endpoints OpenClaw uses: web search and [llm-context](https://docs.openclaw.ai/brave-search).
- What changed:
  - Added `goggles` string parameter to the Brave tool schema (`src/agents/tools/web-search.ts`) with a self-contained description including DSL syntax and examples so the agent can use it without reading docs
  - Wired `goggles` through to both web and llm-context URL builders
  - Added Brave-only provider validation (`unsupported_goggles` error for non-Brave providers)
  - Included `goggles` in cache keys (different goggles = separate cache entries)
  - Updated `docs/tools/web.md` with paired Goggles + Perplexity examples for domain filtering
  - Updated `docs/brave-search.md` with parameter table entry and usage notes
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - Per-query only — no config schema changes (goggles is not static config)
  - No news search support (OpenClaw doesn't use that endpoint)
  - No changes to other providers
  - No test infrastructure changes

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `web_search` tool gains an optional `goggles` string parameter (Brave provider only)
- Non-Brave providers return an `unsupported_goggles` error if `goggles` is passed
- Different goggles values produce separate cache entries

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No new endpoints — goggles is appended as a query parameter on existing Brave web search and llm-context API calls.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Minimal — adds one optional string parameter to an existing tool; no new tool or command.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: Brave Search API
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Brave API key configured

### Steps

1. `pnpm build` — no TS errors
2. `pnpm test -- web-search.test` — all 40 tests pass
3. `pnpm check` — clean
4. Live API: inline goggles (`$discard\n$site=docs.rs\n$site=rust-lang.org`) returned only docs.rs and rust-lang.org results on both web search and llm-context endpoints

### Expected

- Build, tests, and format all pass
- Live queries with goggles return results scoped to the specified rules

### Actual

- All pass as expected

### Examples

```
// Restrict to trusted academic sources
goggles: "$discard\n$site=arxiv.org\n$site=*.edu\n$site=huggingface.co"

// Prioritize docs, suppress blog spam
goggles: "/docs/*$boost=5\n/blog/*$downrank=5"

// Boost actual source code on GitHub
goggles: "$boost=5,site=github.com,inurl=/blob/"
```

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: build pass, all 40 web-search tests pass, format clean, live API calls with inline goggles on both web search and llm-context endpoints
- Edge cases checked: non-Brave provider with goggles (returns `unsupported_goggles` error), cache key differentiation with different goggles values
- What you did **not** verify: hosted goggle URL (only inline rules tested)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — new optional parameter, no breaking changes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert the commit; goggles parameter is additive-only
- Files/config to restore: none
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: `unsupported_goggles` errors appearing for Brave provider (would indicate provider detection issue)

## Risks and Mitigations

Low — additive optional parameter on existing Brave API calls; no behavior change when goggles is not supplied.
